decomposition.title: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
decomposition.industry: Manufacturing and enterprise solutions; kitchen and bath products, wellness, hospitality, and digital innovation
decomposition.estimated_deal_size_usd: No explicit budget stated. Inferred indicative opportunity size: approximately $15M-$50M+ over a likely multi-year hosting transformation horizon, based on enterprise-scale data center exit assessment, large VMware/VxRail and physical server estate, complex SAP ecosystem, databases, backup/DR, network connectivity, monitoring/logging/security tooling, and separate SAP/non-SAP hosting cost requirements. Phase 0 advisory/business-case work alone would be materially smaller, but the RFP requests pricing views for foundational setup, hosting, migration/implementation, consulting, support, and managed services.
decomposition.contract_duration: Not explicitly stated. This is a Phase 0 evaluation initiative, with detailed legal terms, contractual conditions, compliance requirements, and SLAs to be defined in subsequent phases following vendor selection. Inferred for commercial sizing only: likely 3-5 years for any future hosting/DCaaS/hybrid managed services arrangement, but this is not stated in the RFP.

requirements.text: Responses must be complete, clearly structured, and aligned to each requirement.
Vendors are required to complete the response template in detail; simple ‘Yes/No’ answers will not be considered sufficient.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The response template is an external provided artifact; failure to complete it in detail is expressly stated to make simple answers insufficient before scoring.

requirements.text: Proposals must be submitted within the defined timeline via the designated communication channel; RFP responses/proposals are due to Kohler on 26 June.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Submission requirements are split between proposal instructions and the timeline/contact sections; missing the due date or channel can prevent acceptance.

requirements.text: All communication related to this RFP should be directed to Nicholas.Groh@Kohler.com with copy to RFP-Responses-Polaris@kohler.com.
All clarification queries must be submitted within the defined timeline and use the template for Q&A provided to the contact provided.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Clarification compliance requires both timing and a Q&A template; late or incorrectly formatted questions may not be answered.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate the proposed target-state architecture aligned to multi-account / multi-subscription landing zone principles.
Vendors must clearly articulate the landing zone deployment model and application deployment model.
Vendors must clearly articulate environment segregation for Prod / Test / Dev.
Vendors must clearly articulate identity integration with enterprise identity providers.
Vendors must clearly articulate the hybrid connectivity approach.
Vendors must clearly articulate region strategy and data residency approach.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Data residency appears as a single bullet under architecture scope and could materially affect solution viability, but no permitted or prohibited regions are specified.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Region strategy and data residency approach are required, but specific jurisdictions are not defined in the provided document.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly articulate disaster recovery, high availability, and scalability patterns.
Architecture diagrams, reference designs, and supporting documentation are strongly encouraged.
Vendors must respond for relevant scenarios, including Cloud, DCaaS, and Hybrid where applicable, and clearly state assumptions.
Enterprise applications currently hosted in the Kohler Wisconsin Data Center are within scope.
Commercial off-the-shelf and internally developed applications are within scope.
Windows and Linux virtual machines are within scope.
Databases including SQL Server, Oracle, SAP HANA, MySQL, PostgreSQL, and other supported platforms are within scope.

Middleware and integration platforms, file storage systems, shared infrastructure services, backup and disaster recovery solutions, identity and access management integrations, network connectivity services, monitoring, logging, and security tooling are within scope.
Selected OT-adjacent workloads are in scope only for read-only or controlled integration scenarios.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: OT-adjacent workload scope is constrained to read-only or controlled integrations; proposing broader OT control integrations could be non-compliant with scope.

requirements.text: Vendors must base their response on the provided inventory dataset and use it as the baseline for sizing, solution design, and indicative pricing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The inventory baseline is an attached online spreadsheet not included in the provided text; response sizing and pricing must align to it.

requirements.text: Vendors must highlight any data gaps or assumptions made due to incomplete information and clearly state assumptions used for sizing and costing.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: The landscape is subject to regular changes, so vendors must call out gaps and assumptions rather than silently sizing from incomplete data.

requirements.text: Vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards; evidence of certifications is required.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This mandatory certification evidence requirement is located in a short Compliance & Regulatory Requirements subsection and does not list the certifications in the main summary.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry/regional standards are referenced but not specified.
requirements.cert_required: Evidence of certifications required for applicable industry/regional standards.

requirements.text: DCaaS providers must explicitly provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Conditional mandatory DCaaS attestation requirement is buried in the compliance subsection and points to the Vendor response template for more information.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Industry standards are not named in the provided document.
requirements.cert_required: Compliance attestations as per industry standards for DCaaS providers.

requirements.text: Detailed migration execution and implementation services, including actual workload movement, cutover planning, and execution activities, are out of scope.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Detailed migration execution is excluded even though migration/implementation costs are requested later; vendors should avoid overcommitting execution deliverables in the Phase 0 response.

requirements.text: Application refactoring, remediation, or modernization activities, including code changes, re-platforming, and re-architecting, are out of scope.
Detailed low-level design and configuration-level architecture beyond high-level solution design and reference architecture are out of scope.
Operational transition and steady-state managed services delivery commitments, including full-scale IT operations and support models, are out of scope for execution.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Steady-state managed services delivery commitments are out of scope, but managed services costs are requested in pricing guidelines.

requirements.text: Execution of security controls, compliance certification, or regulatory audits is out of scope; vendors are only required to demonstrate capability and readiness.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Vendors must demonstrate capability/readiness, but execution of controls, certifications, or audits is out of scope.

requirements.text: Vendors must clearly state all assumptions, dependencies, and constraints associated with their response.
Any optional services or additional capabilities not covered under the scope must be explicitly identified separately.
Vendors must provide high-level indicative solution designs and supporting explanations where relevant.
Pricing must be transparent, structured, clearly itemized, and aligned to the RFP requirements and inventory baseline.

Vendors must provide comprehensive cost breakup, including foundational setup costs, application and infrastructure hosting costs, migration/implementation costs if applicable, and consulting, support, or managed services costs.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Pricing must include categories that may be interpreted differently because some execution activities are out of scope; vendors should label assumptions clearly.

requirements.text: Vendors must provide separate cost views for SAP and non-SAP workloads, ensuring clear visibility into cost drivers across application categories.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Failure to split SAP and non-SAP pricing can undermine evaluation comparability and cost driver visibility.

requirements.text: All commercial values and pricing submissions must be quoted in United States Dollars (USD).
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Currency mandate is in pricing guidelines rather than submission instructions; non-USD pricing may be rejected as non-comparable.

requirements.text: Proposals should include cost increase protections and efficiency gains that lead to a reduction or no more than a reasonable increase over time, based on prices being paid and not general discounts off list price.
Payment terms are an important part of the proposal; anything less than net 90 payment terms will not be considered competitive, and the proposal should contemplate no less than net 90 payment terms.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Not phrased as automatic rejection, but anything less than net 90 is expressly not competitive and can severely damage commercial scoring.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to provide a high-level overview of their proposed program governance model aligned to the proposed solution.

The governance overview should include indicative governance structure and engagement model, reporting and review mechanisms, escalation framework for issue resolution, and high-level key roles and responsibilities.
Vendors must ensure that all information received from Kohler is used solely for the purpose of responding to this RFP.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Confidentiality obligations are in the appendix and apply to all RFP information, not just final contracting.

requirements.text: Vendors must not disclose, distribute, or share any information with third parties without prior written consent from Kohler.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: This restriction is buried in Legal & Compliance Documentation and can affect use of subcontractors, partners, offshore teams, and external advisors without prior written consent.

requirements.text: Vendors are expected to ensure that their proposed solutions are capable of complying with applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations relevant to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments.
requirements.hidden_risk_reason: Broad compliance/data protection obligation is in the appendix and extends to enterprise IT and manufacturing environments, but the exact regulations are deferred/unspecified.
requirements.jurisdiction_note: Applicable industry standards, regulatory requirements, and data protection obligations are referenced but not specifically listed.

requirements.text: Kohler reserves the right to modify, amend, or withdraw this RFP at any stage; request additional information or clarification from vendors; and accept or reject any proposal without obligation or liability.

similar_deals.title: Kohler Co. Project Polaris Hosting Options Evaluation
similar_deals.deal_size_usd: No explicit budget; inferred $15M-$50M+ downstream, with Phase 0 advisory options at $279,533-$595,000 and a 36-month modernization option at $30,667,392
similar_deals.outcome: Unknown / work in progress, not evidence of a win
similar_deals.why_relevant: Direct match to the current RFP title, client, geography, industry, data center exit scope, cloud/DCaaS/hybrid evaluation, SAP and non-SAP hosting cost requirements, and Phase 0 advisory/business-case framing.
similar_deals.key_lesson: The strongest proposal pattern is a vendor-neutral Hosting Option Control Tower / Hosting Decision Lab with open-book USD TCO, workload placement, SAP/VMware dependency analysis, target landing zone, hybrid connectivity, DR, FinOps, and vendor coexistence governance.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation, Germany
similar_deals.why_relevant: Highly relevant manufacturing transformation in the target commercial band. Supports a win theme around rapid Phase 0 mobilisation, manufacturing credibility, and readiness to move from assessment into execution/managed services.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won because of fastest mobilisation, team readiness within 2 weeks, and strongest manufacturing domain credentials.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation, UK
similar_deals.why_relevant: Very close in industry, transformation nature, and deal size. Directly relevant because Kohler’s RFP contains eliminatory template, submission, communication, certification, USD pricing, and confidentiality requirements.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost due to a missed local entity requirement in the compliance appendix, proving that administrative and legal compliance can decide similar manufacturing transformation pursuits.

similar_deals.title: Manufacturing IT Transformation, Netherlands
similar_deals.why_relevant: Similar manufacturing transformation and commercial size. Reinforces the need for a red-team compliance workstream before bidding.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Lost because a local entity requirement was missed in the compliance appendix, reinforcing that compliance appendices must be treated as scored gates, not back-office paperwork.

similar_deals.title: SAP S/4HANA Global Rollout, Germany/USA/Mexico
similar_deals.why_relevant: Larger than the inferred Polaris range but relevant because Kohler has a complex SAP ecosystem and global footprint. Supports dedicated SAP hosting, migration, downtime-risk, and follow-the-sun support themes.
similar_deals.key_lesson: Won using SAP accelerator, zero-downtime methodology, and global follow-the-sun delivery against major competitors.

win_intel.win_probability_rationale: Estimated win probability is 38%. This is intentionally conservative. Positive evidence: there is a direct past-proposal match for Kohler Project Polaris with highly relevant solution patterns; the deal corpus includes a comparable manufacturing transformation win, DEAL-089, at $22M where rapid mobilisation and domain credentials won; and DEAL-003 shows proven SAP/global transformation credibility relevant to Kohler’s SAP ecosystem. Negative evidence: the direct Kohler proposal is work-in-progress and not a proven win; there is no public procurement award data to validate competitor pricing or actual winners; similar manufacturing deals show two losses, DEAL-056 and DEAL-100, both caused by compliance appendix misses; the current RFP has multiple eliminatory/mandatory requirements around template completion, submission channel, certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, and confidentiality; and prior intelligence suggests a possible incumbent advantage for Cognizant or another established provider. Because public procurement data is unavailable, confidence is medium-low despite having two useful internal intelligence layers. If certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, template compliance, and Kohler-specific architecture/pricing are completed and if incumbent advantage is weak, probability could improve. If the incumbent is strongly favored or compliance evidence is incomplete, probability should fall below 25%.
capability_gaps: Compliance certification evidence is not confirmed in the provided intelligence, yet the RFP states that vendors must demonstrate capability to host workloads in compliance with applicable industry/regional standards and provide evidence of certifications. This must be supplied or the bid risks disqualification.

If proposing DCaaS, DCaaS compliance attestations are not evidenced in the provided intelligence, while the RFP explicitly requires DCaaS providers to provide compliance attestations as per industry standards.

The intelligence contains strong recommended architecture patterns, but no finalized Kohler-specific target-state diagrams, region/data-residency model, identity integration design, hybrid connectivity design, or DR/HA/scalability design. These are mandatory RFP requirements and must be converted into detailed, Kohler-specific response content.

The provided intelligence does not confirm possession/completion of Kohler’s required response template. The RFP states that detailed completion of the template is mandatory and simple Yes/No answers are insufficient.

The RFP requires pricing in USD across foundational setup, hosting, migration/implementation, consulting, support, and managed services. Prior proposal content contains useful Phase 0 and downstream benchmarks, but a complete RFP-compliant commercial model by SAP/non-SAP, cloud/DCaaS/hybrid, environment, SLA, backup/DR, and support tier is not yet evidenced.
recommended_win_themes: Evidence-led Hosting Option Control Tower: position Polaris as a vendor-neutral decision factory that gives Kohler an owned, defensible workload-by-workload business case across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration rather than a sales-led migration recommendation.

Manufacturing-safe SAP and data center exit: emphasize continuity for SAP/ERP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, plants, hospitality operations, connected products, analytics, and customer-facing digital experiences, with explicit downtime, rollback, DR, and hypercare controls.

Rapid mobilisation with named Phase 0 plan: use the DEAL-089 lesson by committing to a two-week-or-faster mobilisation model and a structured 12-week Hosting Decision Lab with clear workstreams for infrastructure, SAP, network, security, FinOps, operations, and vendor governance.

Accelerator-driven risk reduction: show reusable IP for data center exit assessment, VMware/VxRail discovery, SAP dependency mapping, application-to-infrastructure mapping, migration-wave planning, hosting TCO modeling, backup/DR assessment, and security/logging integration.

Transparent USD economics and vendor coexistence: provide open-book TCO with separate SAP/non-SAP and cloud/DCaaS/hybrid views, FinOps guardrails, licensing assumptions, run-cost baselines, and a RACI for coexistence with incumbents and future providers.
risk_factors: No public procurement award data is available, so competitor pricing and win-rate assumptions cannot be validated against government award ground truth. This lowers confidence in the win probability.

The strongest past-proposal match is a work-in-progress proposal for the same Kohler Project Polaris opportunity, not a completed win, so it proves relevance of solution thinking but not buyer acceptance.

Deal corpus shows mixed outcomes in similar manufacturing transformations: DEAL-089 was won, but DEAL-056 and DEAL-100 were lost due to compliance misses. This RFP has multiple eliminatory and mandatory procedural requirements, making compliance a major bid risk.

Prior proposal intelligence indicates Cognizant may be an incumbent strategic partner across data center operations, cloud platforms, observability, cybersecurity, and digital services. If true, incumbent advantage should materially reduce win probability unless the bid differentiates as more vendor-neutral, transparent, and lower-risk.

The RFP is Phase 0, but the inferred downstream opportunity is large. Kohler may use the evaluation to benchmark providers before selecting an incumbent, hyperscaler-aligned partner, DCaaS provider, or top-tier hybrid-cloud advisory firm for later phases.
No explicit budget or duration is stated. Commercial misalignment is possible if the response over-rotates toward a $30M+ transformation before first winning the advisory/business-case phase.
Mandatory certification evidence, DCaaS attestations, USD pricing, confidentiality controls, and template completion are disqualification risks if not handled exactly.
must_fix_before_bidding: Complete Kohler’s provided response template in full detail and map every one of the 44 requirements to a compliance matrix; do not rely on generic narrative or Yes/No answers.
Submit all pricing in USD and separate Phase 0 advisory, foundational setup, hosting/run, migration/implementation, consulting, support, managed services, SAP hosting, and non-SAP hosting cost views.
Attach certification evidence for relevant hosting, cloud, security, data protection, and operational standards; if any DCaaS option is included, attach explicit DCaaS compliance attestations.

Create Kohler-specific target-state architecture content covering multi-account/multi-subscription landing zones, landing-zone deployment model, application deployment model, Prod/Test/Dev segregation, identity integration, hybrid connectivity, region/data residency, DR, HA, scalability, observability, backup, logging, and security tooling.

Run a red-team compliance review against submission deadline, communication channel, clarification process, confidentiality/third-party sharing restriction, legal deviations, local/entity requirements, insurance, data residency, and subcontractor disclosures, because two highly similar manufacturing transformation losses were caused by missed compliance appendix requirements.

Includes a comprehensive 12-week decision lab, detailed Kohler-owned target architecture, enhanced compliance evidence, full migration factory, 24/7 SRE-led operations, stronger DR/backup design, expanded transition governance, and higher-touch SAP/non-SAP run support.
key_components: Fixed-fee premium Phase 0 Hosting Decision Lab with cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS evaluation
Kohler-owned landing zone, identity, network, region, DR/HA, security, observability and compliance architecture package
Dedicated migration factory for SAP, VMware, physical servers, databases, backup/DR and monitoring/logging dependencies
24/7 managed services with expanded SRE, DBA, network, security, service delivery and FinOps coverage
Enhanced parallel-run, rollback, hypercare and incumbent transition governance
Higher infrastructure allowance for resilient hybrid connectivity, DR, backup, security tooling and platform licensing
solution_options.rationale: Lowest-risk option for Kohler if the priority is resilience, comprehensive compliance evidence, stronger transition control and reduced operational disruption. Pricing math: Phase 0 $0.850M + People $16.800M + Infrastructure $8.700M + Migration $4.800M + Governance $2.650M = $33.800M subtotal; +15% contingency $5.070M = $38.870M cost; +22% gross margin $10.963M = $49.833M total. This is above the competitor price-to-win range but remains within the RFP-inferred $15M-$50M+ enterprise opportunity size because it includes a more fully bundled, lower-risk operating model.

solution_options.name: Balanced Polaris Decision Factory plus Hybrid Modernization Control Tower
solution_options.description: Balanced recommended option: vendor-neutral Polaris decision factory followed by a practical 36-month Hybrid Modernization Control Tower. Covers Phase 0 evaluation, landing zone setup, migration/implementation, cloud/DCaaS/hybrid hosting allowance, managed services, governance, FinOps, security, observability and incumbent transition management.
key_components: 12-week fixed-fee Polaris Hosting Decision Lab aligned to HCLTech's prior Kohler-specific proposal benchmark
Open-book USD TCO model comparing cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge and SaaS integration options
Azure-first / cloud-agnostic landing zone blueprint with multi-account or multi-subscription governance
SAP and non-SAP workload placement scorecards, migration waves, rollback gates and implementation roadmap
36-month co-managed hybrid operations with SRE, security, network, DBA, service delivery, ITSM and FinOps roles
Compliance-attestation matrix, DR test blueprint, observability integration and vendor coexistence RACI
solution_options.rationale: Recommended because it directly follows HCLTech's most relevant prior Polaris pricing anchor of $30.667M for a 36-month co-managed hybrid modernization/control-tower program and fits the competitor price-to-win range of $18M-$34M for downstream transformation/run services. Pricing math: Phase 0 $0.595M + People $11.300M + Infrastructure $6.200M + Migration $2.900M + Governance $1.184M = $22.179M subtotal; +12% contingency $2.662M = $24.841M cost; +19% gross margin $5.827M = $30.667M total.

solution_options.name: Lean Co-managed Hosting Evaluation and Selective Modernization
solution_options.description: Lean lowest-cost model using a fixed-fee Phase 0, selective migration enablement, co-managed operations and heavier reliance on Kohler/incumbent teams and existing tooling. It still covers the complete evaluation, setup, migration support, hosting allowance and managed services scope, but with narrower service levels, leaner staffing and higher delivery dependency on Kohler and incumbent providers.
key_components: Lean Phase 0 decision lab focused on minimum viable workload placement, TCO and target-state architecture outputs
Selective landing zone and migration support rather than full migration factory ownership
Co-managed run model with smaller HCLTech operations team and greater use of Kohler/incumbent staff
Use of existing monitoring, ITSM, backup and security tooling where commercially and technically feasible
Reduced parallel-run/hypercare allowance and fewer dedicated specialist roles
Lower infrastructure allowance with final cloud/DCaaS/telecom/license true-up required after inventory validation
solution_options.rationale: Lowest-cost option designed to remain competitive against aggressive bidders, but it carries higher transition, schedule and operational-risk exposure because of reduced staffing, lighter hypercare and stronger dependency on Kohler/incumbent execution. Pricing math: Phase 0 $0.400M + People $7.750M + Infrastructure $4.400M + Migration $2.550M + Governance $0.860M = $15.960M subtotal; +10% contingency $1.596M = $17.556M cost; +16% gross margin $3.344M = $20.900M total.

pricing.competitive_rationale: Recommended price is anchored to HCLTech's most relevant past Polaris pricing pattern: a 36-month co-managed hybrid modernization / Hosting Option Control Tower at $30,667,392, with Phase 0 advisory benchmarks of $279,533-$595,000. Competitor price-to-win intelligence indicates $18M-$34M for full multi-year transformation/run services excluding hyperscaler/DCaaS/telecom/license pass-throughs, and $400k-$850k for Phase 0. Option B stays within that competitive range while pricing the complete Phase 0 + migration/implementation + 36-month managed services scope. No public contract award anchors were provided, and live Azure/AWS unit pricing APIs were unavailable; therefore infrastructure is modeled as an enterprise-scale hosting/cloud/DCaaS/backup/DR/security allowance calibrated from the Polaris past proposal rather than unit-priced from cloud APIs. Confidence is therefore held below 0.5.

draft.rfp_id: Project Polaris: Evaluation of Hosting Options (Cloud, DCaaS & Hybrid)
draft.executive_summary:
Kohler’s Project Polaris should not be treated as a binary cloud-versus-data-center exercise. It is a resilience, cost, architecture, security, and operating-model decision for a global manufacturer whose Wisconsin Data Center, plants, hospitality operations, connected products, analytics platforms, SAP estate, and customer digital experiences must continue to perform while the future hosting model is evaluated.

This aligns directly with Eric Tabor’s stated priorities to use technology for operational and business excellence, improve productivity, and respond faster to business needs, and with Paul Ryan’s 2024 emphasis on protecting connected-product privacy as part of brand trust. HCLTech proposes to turn Polaris into a contractually vendor-neutral, Kohler-owned Phase 0 decision factory: a 12-week Hosting Decision Lab that compares public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration workload by workload.

Our approach gives Kohler control, not lock-in. We will baseline workloads, SAP and VMware/VxRail dependencies, physical servers, network paths, security controls, RTO/RPO, observability, contracts, run costs, and incumbent-provider accountabilities. We will then produce open-book USD TCO, workload placement scorecards, target landing-zone diagrams, migration waves, rollback gates, DR/security/observability designs, a vendor-coexistence RACI, and a compliance-attestation matrix Kohler may use with any downstream provider. The result is an evidence-led Hosting Option Control Tower that protects manufacturing continuity while giving leadership a defensible platform choice.
win_themes: Evidence-led Hosting Option Control Tower: a vendor-neutral decision factory giving Kohler an owned, defensible workload-by-workload business case across public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration.

Manufacturing-safe SAP and data center exit: continuity for SAP/ERP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, plants, hospitality operations, connected products, analytics, and customer-facing digital experiences with downtime, rollback, DR, and hypercare controls.

Rapid mobilisation with named Phase 0 plan: two-week-or-faster mobilisation and a structured 12-week Hosting Decision Lab across infrastructure, SAP, network, security, FinOps, operations, and vendor governance.

Accelerator-driven risk reduction: reusable IP for data center exit assessment, VMware/VxRail discovery, SAP dependency mapping, application-to-infrastructure mapping, migration-wave planning, hosting TCO modeling, backup/DR assessment, and security/logging integration.

Transparent USD economics and vendor coexistence: open-book SAP and non-SAP TCO for cloud, DCaaS, and hybrid options, FinOps guardrails, licensing assumptions, run-cost baselines, and RACI-based coexistence with incumbents and future providers.

sections.section_title: Target state architecture, security and governance capabilities
sections.content:
The target state begins with a neutral workload-placement framework, not a preselected destination. HCLTech will establish the Hosting Option Control Tower as the architecture, security, and governance mechanism for Polaris. Each application, platform, database, integration, and infrastructure service will be evaluated against business criticality, latency, data classification, RTO/RPO, SAP dependency, VMware/VxRail constraint, physical-server requirement, plant/edge proximity, hospitality impact, connected-product privacy, licensing, and operational ownership.

The Control Tower will produce reference architectures for public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration. For cloud patterns, we will define an Azure-first landing-zone blueprint that remains extensible to multi-cloud and hybrid operations, including identity integration, segmentation, encryption, policy-as-code, logging, monitoring, backup, disaster recovery, FinOps guardrails, and AIOps integration. For DCaaS and retained data center patterns, we will define facility, compute, storage, network, backup, monitoring, and governance controls with comparable compliance evidence.

Security governance will be expressed through a compliance-attestation matrix, shared-responsibility model, access-control baseline, logging requirements, third-party data-sharing restrictions, and architecture decision records. This evidence-led architecture gives Kohler a reusable target-state blueprint and preserves long-term provider choice.

sections.section_title: Migration approach, risk considerations and transition strategy
sections.content:
Polaris will de-risk future execution before any migration commitment is made. During the 12-week Phase 0 Hosting Decision Lab, we will mobilize in two weeks or faster, confirm governance, secure data access, and stand up workstreams for infrastructure, SAP, network, security, FinOps, operations, and vendor governance. Weeks 2–4 baseline the estate; weeks 5–7 evaluate hosting scenarios; weeks 8–9 design the target state; weeks 10–11 build the business case; week 12 confirms the executive decision package.

Migration strategy will be defined by workload waves, not technology towers alone. We will group SAP/ERP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, databases, analytics, hospitality systems, connected-product platforms, plant/edge services, and customer-facing digital experiences according to dependency maps, outage tolerance, regulatory controls, technical debt, licensing impact, and business calendars. Each wave will include entry criteria, exit criteria, rehearsal needs, rollback method, DR validation, performance gates, application-owner sign-off, and hypercare model.

Incumbent transition governance is explicit. The RACI will separate Kohler, incumbent, HCLTech, DCaaS, cloud, telecom, and security responsibilities so migration planning complements current services while protecting Kohler’s control of downstream choices.

Kohler’s hosting decision cannot be separated from network behavior across Wisconsin, global plants, hospitality properties, cloud regions, SaaS platforms, support partners, and connected-product ecosystems. The Control Tower will therefore baseline WAN, LAN, internet, private connectivity, VPN, DNS, firewall, load-balancing, proxy, identity, and remote-access patterns before recommending placement. Latency-sensitive plant, SAP, database, and hospitality workloads will be assessed using measured dependencies rather than generic cloud assumptions.

We will create target connectivity patterns for each option: retained data center, DCaaS interconnect, public cloud landing zone, hybrid cloud, plant/edge, and SaaS integration. Designs will cover segmentation, routing, bandwidth, ingress/egress cost drivers, certificate and DNS ownership, firewall rule rationalization, privileged access, third-party connectivity, and monitoring handoffs. Where cloud is viable, the blueprint will define private connectivity, hub-and-spoke or equivalent network topology, inspection points, identity integration, and logging paths. Where DCaaS or retained data center is stronger, the same evidence model will define cross-connects, carrier resilience, remote access, and operational accountability.

The outcome is a network decision pack that makes hosting economics, security exposure, latency, and transition risk visible before Kohler commits to any provider.

sections.section_title: Availability, resilience and disaster recovery approach
sections.content:
Manufacturing-safe hosting requires availability evidence, not aspirational uptime language. HCLTech will baseline current RTO, RPO, backup success, replication, clustering, maintenance windows, monitoring coverage, incident history, DR-test evidence, and business-calendar constraints for SAP/ERP, VMware/VxRail, physical servers, databases, plant/edge systems, hospitality operations, analytics, and connected-product services. This creates a factual resilience baseline for comparing cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, and hybrid options.

For each workload placement scenario, we will define resilience patterns, including availability zones or facility pairs, backup architecture, immutable backup options, replication approach, recovery sequencing, runbook ownership, restoration testing, cyber-recovery considerations, and observability integration. We will identify workloads that require low-latency local survivability, workloads that can tolerate regional recovery, and workloads where SaaS or managed-platform resilience should be contractually evidenced.

The Decision Lab will deliver a DR and resiliency blueprint with workload-tiering, RTO/RPO targets, test cadence, recovery dependencies, rollback gates, and hypercare controls. By making continuity criteria part of the placement scorecard, Polaris protects Kohler’s SAP, plant, hospitality, and customer experience operations while enabling a practical target state.

Cost transparency is central to the Polaris win theme: Kohler should own the economics before selecting any hosting path. We propose a fixed-price 12-week Phase 0 Hosting Decision Lab for USD 279,533.42, using open-book assumptions and no third-party data sharing without Kohler’s written consent. Optional downstream modernization can be priced later by approved waves, avoiding any implication that the assessment forces HCLTech-managed migration pull-through.

The TCO model will separate SAP and non-SAP costs and compare public cloud, DCaaS, retained data center, hybrid, plant/edge, and SaaS integration views. It will distinguish one-time transition, recurring run, facilities, compute, storage, network, backup, DR, security tooling, observability, licensing, labor, telecom, cloud consumption, DCaaS charges, managed services, and decommissioning. FinOps guardrails will document tagging, budgets, unit economics, reserved-capacity assumptions, rightsizing, egress sensitivity, showback/chargeback options, and optimization cadence.

Commercial outputs will include a USD cost baseline, scenario TCO, sensitivity analysis, licensing assumptions, risk-adjusted migration cost, and provider-comparable sourcing pack. This gives Kohler defensible economics and vendor coexistence without hidden lock-in.

The operating model must support hybrid reality from day one. HCLTech will document current responsibilities across Kohler teams, Cognizant and other incumbents, telecom carriers, data center providers, security operations, cloud platform teams, application owners, and business stakeholders. The output will be a vendor-coexistence RACI that clarifies decision rights, incident ownership, change control, access approvals, monitoring handoffs, escalation paths, FinOps accountability, DR runbook ownership, and audit evidence responsibilities.

The proposed future operating model will be ITIL-aligned and adapted for manufacturing, hospitality, connected products, and global enterprise platforms. We will define service towers for infrastructure, SAP basis coordination, cloud/DCaaS operations, network, security, backup/DR, observability, endpoint and plant/edge coordination, vendor management, and service integration. AIOps and observability integration will be designed so alerts, logs, metrics, events, capacity signals, and security telemetry can feed Kohler’s evolving AI-driven operations without fragmenting accountability.

Support deliverables will include governance cadence, operational KPIs, hypercare model, runbook standards, service transition checklist, knowledge-transfer plan, and retained-organization recommendations. This operating model reinforces Kohler control while allowing multiple providers to coexist safely.

Tool maturity will reduce assessment risk and shorten the path from evidence to decision. HCLTech will apply reusable accelerators for data center exit assessment, VMware/VxRail discovery, application-to-infrastructure mapping, SAP dependency mapping, migration-wave planning, hosting TCO modeling, backup/DR assessment, and security/logging integration. These accelerators structure the work; Kohler’s actual inventory, performance, cost, contract, and security evidence will drive the recommendations.

The tool ecosystem will support discovery, dependency visualization, cost modeling, architecture decision records, RAID management, regulatory evidence, and executive reporting. Where Kohler already has observability, security, asset, CMDB, cloud, or Neuro IT Ops tooling, we will integrate rather than replace by default. The objective is to create a common evidence layer that can be used by Kohler, incumbents, cloud providers, DCaaS providers, and future implementation partners under Kohler-approved access controls.

Platform maturity will be measured through scorecards covering automation readiness, policy controls, identity integration, logging completeness, backup recoverability, network resilience, FinOps capability, operational supportability, and compliance evidence. This accelerator-driven approach makes Polaris faster, repeatable, auditable, and vendor-neutral.

Our deliverable set is designed so Kohler can proceed, pause, compete, or redirect after Phase 0 without losing value. All artifacts will be Kohler-owned and usable with HCLTech, the incumbent, Infosys, Capgemini, TCS, Accenture, cloud providers, DCaaS providers, or any other downstream partner. This contractual portability is the core of the evidence-led Hosting Option Control Tower.

The 12-week Hosting Decision Lab will deliver a mobilization plan, stakeholder map, RACI, RAID log, data request list, current-state workload and infrastructure baseline, SAP and VMware/VxRail dependency maps, application-to-infrastructure mapping, cost baseline, security and logging baseline, RTO/RPO inventory, workload placement scorecards, target architecture options, landing-zone diagrams, network design, DR and resiliency blueprint, compliance-attestation matrix, AIOps/observability integration plan, vendor-coexistence model, migration-wave roadmap, rollback gates, hypercare approach, open-book USD TCO, FinOps guardrails, assumptions register, and executive decision package.

Every deliverable will trace to a decision: where to host, how to secure, how to connect, how to recover, how to operate, what it costs, and who is accountable. That traceability makes Polaris a defensible decision factory.
